title: mRNA Cancer Vaccines: Fighting Tumors with Your Own DNA

slides.heading: mRNA Cancer Vaccines
slides.body: How a pandemic-era breakthrough is now being turned against one of humanity's oldest enemies: cancer

slides.heading: From COVID to Cancer
slides.body: The mRNA vaccines that defeated COVID-19 proved the technology works in humans. Researchers immediately pivoted: could the same platform teach the immune system to hunt tumor cells? The answer, it turns out, is yes.

slides.heading: What Makes Cancer Hard to Vaccinate Against
slides.body: Unlike viruses, cancer cells arise from your own body. They disguise themselves as 'self,' evading immune detection. Traditional vaccines target foreign pathogens — cancer required an entirely new approach.

slides.heading: The Neoantigen Strategy
slides.body: Tumors accumulate unique mutations that produce altered proteins called neoantigens — molecular flags that mark them as abnormal. mRNA vaccines are designed to display these flags, training T-cells to recognize and destroy cells bearing them.

slides.heading: Personalized to Each Patient
slides.body: Doctors biopsy the patient's tumor and sequence its DNA. An algorithm selects the strongest neoantigen targets, and a custom mRNA vaccine is synthesized in about six weeks. Every dose is unique — built from the patient's own cancer blueprint.

slides.heading: Melanoma Breakthrough
slides.body: A 2023 Moderna/Merck Phase 2 trial showed a personalized mRNA vaccine combined with Keytruda reduced the risk of melanoma recurrence by 49% versus immunotherapy alone. It was the first randomized trial to prove mRNA vaccines can fight solid tumors.

slides.heading: Taking On Pancreatic Cancer
slides.body: Pancreatic cancer has a five-year survival rate under 12%. In 2023, a Memorial Sloan Kettering trial showed personalized mRNA vaccines triggered strong T-cell responses in half of patients — and those responders went longer without relapse.

slides.heading: Supercharged by Combination Therapy
slides.body: mRNA vaccines work even better alongside checkpoint inhibitors like Keytruda, which 'take the brakes off' immune cells. The combination creates a one-two punch: the vaccine identifies targets, and the inhibitor keeps T-cells attacking aggressively.

slides.heading: The Challenges Ahead
slides.body: Personalized manufacturing is expensive and slow — currently around six weeks per patient. Scaling production, reducing cost, and extending the approach to more cancer types (lung, colorectal, breast) are the major hurdles researchers are racing to solve.

slides.heading: A New Era of Cancer Treatment
slides.body: Phase 3 trials for melanoma are underway, with results expected by 2026. If they hold, mRNA cancer vaccines could become a standard therapy — turning each patient's unique tumor mutations into the very weapon used to destroy it.
